name: Pakhet
description: Pakhet, also known as Pakht, Pachet, or Pasht, is an ancient Egyptian lioness goddess of war, hunting, and the desert. Her name translates to 'She Who Tears', reflecting her fierce nature and predatory skills. She was primarily worshipped in Middle Egypt, particularly at Beni Hasan, where her temple, Speos Artemidos, was carved into the cliffs. Pakhet was often associated with protecting the pharaoh and ensuring successful hunts. She was seen as a fierce protector, capable of destroying enemies and defending the innocent. Her connection to the desert emphasized her wild, untamed power. Pakhet was also linked to the sun and the moon, embodying both solar and lunar aspects. She was sometimes considered a local form of Sekhmet or Bastet, sharing similar feline attributes and protective roles, but maintaining her unique identity and characteristics. Her worship involved rituals and offerings to appease her power and secure her favor, particularly in matters of protection and success in hunting. She was seen as a powerful force to be reckoned with, commanding respect and invoking fear in those who dared to challenge her. Pakhet's dual nature as both a fierce warrior and a protective mother made her a complex and revered figure in the Egyptian pantheon.
subtitle: The Lioness Who Tears

keyMyths.title: Protection of the Pharaoh
keyMyths.description: Pakhet was believed to protect the pharaoh in battle, ensuring his victory and safety. Her fierce nature made her a formidable guardian against enemies of the state.

keyMyths.title: The Huntress
keyMyths.description: Pakhet's association with hunting made her a patron of hunters, ensuring successful expeditions and plentiful game. Offerings were made to her to secure a fruitful hunt.

keyMyths.title: Lunar Association
keyMyths.description: Pakhet's connection to the moon linked her to the cycles of nature and the mysteries of the night. She was seen as a powerful lunar deity alongside her solar aspects.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: Emily Teeter
sources.work: Religion and Ritual in Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Exploration of religious practices and beliefs.
sources.text: Exploration of religious practices and beliefs.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food, drink, and precious items were made to Pakhet in her temples, particularly at Speos Artemidos, to appease her fierce nature and seek her protection. Priests and priestesses conducted rituals and ceremonies to honor the goddess.

worshipPractices.name: Statues and Images
worshipPractices.description: Statues and images of Pakhet, often depicted as a lioness or a woman with the head of a lioness, were venerated. These representations served as focal points for worship and were believed to embody the goddess's presence.

festivals.name: Festival at Speos Artemidos
festivals.description: A major festival was held annually at Speos Artemidos to celebrate Pakhet. This event likely involved processions, music, dancing, and the presentation of offerings to the goddess. The festival served to reinforce Pakhet's importance and strengthen the community's connection to her.

cultural.modernLegacy: While not actively worshipped today, Pakhet's imagery and symbolism occasionally appear in modern art, literature, and neopagan practices related to ancient Egyptian deities. She represents fierce protection and the power of the wild.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Pakhet's origins are somewhat obscure, with her worship primarily localized in Middle Egypt. She is believed to have emerged as a regional deity, possibly predating some of the more widely known Egyptian gods. Her association with lions, powerful predators in the desert environment, suggests a connection to the natural world and the dangers it held. The exact circumstances of her 'birth' or emergence into the Egyptian pantheon are not explicitly detailed in surviving texts, but her fierce nature and role as a protector suggest she was always perceived as a powerful force. Her epithet, 'She Who Tears,' further emphasizes her primal strength and association with hunting and warfare. The etymology of her name reinforces the idea of a deity linked to raw power and destruction, essential qualities for a protector goddess in a challenging environment.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Pakhet does not feature prominently in many well-known Egyptian myths, her role as a protector goddess is consistently emphasized. One story links her to the tale of Sekhmet, the lioness goddess of war. Some accounts suggest Pakhet may have been seen as a more localized or regional manifestation of Sekhmet, or perhaps a gentler aspect of the fierce warrior goddess. Another narrative connects her to the story of Hatshepsut, the female pharaoh who renovated and expanded the temple of Speos Artemidos. Hatshepsut's dedication to Pakhet was likely intended to legitimize her rule and invoke the goddess's protection for her kingdom. These stories, though not as elaborate as those of other deities, highlight Pakhet's importance as a powerful guardian and a symbol of royal authority.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Pakhet's primary cult center was at Speos Artemidos, near Beni Hasan in Middle Egypt. The temple, carved into the cliffs, was dedicated to her and featured numerous chambers and sanctuaries. Worship at Speos Artemidos likely involved daily rituals, offerings of food and drink, and the veneration of statues and images of the goddess. Priests and priestesses played a crucial role in maintaining the cult and performing the necessary ceremonies. The temple also served as a site for festivals and celebrations in Pakhet's honor. The presence of catacombs containing mummified cats near the temple suggests a connection between Pakhet and feline animals, further reinforcing her association with lions and her role as a protector. The strategic location of the temple, overlooking the desert landscape, emphasized Pakhet's dominion over the wild and her ability to safeguard those who sought her protection.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Pakhet's legacy, while not as widespread as that of some other major Egyptian deities, is evident in the continued veneration of her at Speos Artemidos and the preservation of her image in art and religious texts. Her association with lions and her role as a protector goddess have resonated throughout Egyptian history. The temple of Speos Artemidos stands as a testament to her enduring importance and the devotion of her followers. In modern times, Pakhet remains a figure of interest to scholars of ancient Egyptian religion and those drawn to the symbolism of powerful female deities. Her image occasionally appears in contemporary art and literature, serving as a reminder of the rich and complex mythology of ancient Egypt. She represents the fierce protective power of the natural world.

shortDescription: Pakhet is an Egyptian lioness goddess of war, hunting, and the desert, known as 'She Who Tears'. Worshipped primarily in Middle Egypt.
